frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Neha, I've been noticing something, aur main soch raha tha ki tumse baat karoon is baare mein. Office jaate waqt, you're always so well-presented, you put on light makeup, your hair is done... and I really like that, it shows you're professional. But then, weekends par ya shaam ko ghar pe, you just... don't bother with any of it. I mean, thoda effort mere liye bhi toh banta hai na? It makes me feel like the effort is only for the outside world, for your colleagues, for show. Like you don't care about looking a certain way for *me*, in our own home.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as Vikram imposes an expectation on his wife's appearance for his benefit, implying her care for him is linked to her grooming efforts at home. It encodes the surface manifestation of 'demands for compliance with gendered aesthetic standards,' specifically for a wife's appearance within the home.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 35. Married for three years. A man in Mumbai. You are having a serious conversation with your wife Neha at home. You have noticed that Neha wears makeup every day to office but when she is at home with you on weekends she does not put in the same effort. You are telling Neha how this makes you feel and what you think it means about who she is making the effort for and what a wife should look like for her husband at home versus in public.
